clinical_history: 71-year-old woman whom you see on a consult service.  To which unit are you likely heading
diagnosis: Pulmonary. The ECG shows sinus tachycardia (about 100/min) at rest with peaked P waves (at least 2.5 mm in amplitude) in lead 2 ("P pulmonale" pattern) and an inferior-rightward P wave axis (about +80 degrees; note the negative P waves in lead aVL). However, the QRS limb lead voltage is only relatively, but not absolutely low, and slow R wave progression is not seen. The QRS axis is about +45-50 degrees, whiich is within normal limits.

report.axis: Normal QRS axis (+45-50 degrees); inferior-rightward P-wave axis (+80 degrees)
report.intervals: PR: ~160 ms, QRS: ~80 ms, QTc: ~390 ms
report.findings: Peaked P waves (at least 2.5 mm in amplitude) in lead II (P pulmonale pattern); negative P waves in lead aVL; relatively low (but not absolutely low) QRS limb lead voltage; normal R-wave progression.
report.impression: Sinus tachycardia with P pulmonale, highly suggestive of chronic pulmonary disease (e.g., COPD or emphysema).
